coredata.dc:title: Salting-out as a scalable, in-series purification method of graphene oxides from microsheets to quantum dots
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2013 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Ryu, Seongwoo
Lee, Bin
Hong, Seonki
Jin, Sunghwan
Park, Sungjin
Hong, Soon Hyung
Lee, Haeshin
coredata.dc:description: Chemically modified graphenes (CMGs) are used to reinforce composites and as platforms for sensing, electrodes in batteries, conductive films for displays, and others. However, performance of CMG materials is often limited by their physicochemical heterogeneity. Here, we report that simple addition of ammonium sulfate to heterogeneous CMG mixtures, results in effective, scalable, and in-series purification, by salting-out precipitation. The mechanism is based on differences in the dispersibility of the individual graphene oxide (GO). At relatively low ammonium sulfate concentrations (∼10mM), large, GOs with predominant hydrophobic domains are precipitated. As the salt concentration increases, smaller and less GOs with predominant hydrophobic domains are purified, and eventually graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are isolated. The method described herein was inspired by a well-known protein purification process called ‘salting-out,’ in which proteins of different sizes and surface charge, successively become indispersible over a narrow range of salt concentrations. The salting-out process for GOs is simple and scalable, and can be used to achieve large-scale purification.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and suspension of graphene oxide (GO)
Separation of GO and GQD from the suspension

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Ministry of Education, Science and Technology
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Education, Science and Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the financial support from the National Research Foundation of South Korea: World Class University Program (R31-10071, H.L.), Molecular-level Interface Research Center (2012-0000909, H.L.), and Korea Biotech R&D Program (2011K000809, H.L.). This research was supported by the NSL (National Space Lab.) program through the Korea Science and Engineering Foundation funded by the Ministry of Education, Science and Technology (2008-2003213, S.H.H) and the Inha University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the salting-out mechanism and in-series separation of various GOs and GQDs. (a) Diagrammatic representation of the salting-out of GO. Salting-out is the result of the preferential solvating power of water away from their role as solvent. (b) Fabrication step in which the GOs and GQDs are sorted from the as-prepared suspension. In solutions containing low concentrations of salt, less-dispersible GOs, with relatively few functional groups, salt-out first. In solutions with high concentrations of salt, the remaining GOs begin to salt-out; the final supernatant containing the GQDs is separated by removing the salt.

Size distribution of the GO separated by salting-out. The scale bar at the right indicates the size scale of the salted-out GO20p (red), GO50p from GO20s (blue) and GO50s (green). Typical corresponding size distributions of precipitated (a) GO20p; (b) its AFM image; (c) GO50p from GO20s; (d) its AFM image; (e) GO50s from GO20s after removing GO50p; and (f) its AFM image.

Physicochemical characterization of GOs purified by in-series, salting-out from a GO mixture. (a) A photograph of salting-out at various concentrations. From left, pure dispersed GO in DI water, GO5s, GO10s, GO20s, GO30s, GO40s, GO50s, and GO100s. (b) UV–Vis spectra of pure dispersed GO in DI water (red), GO2s (blue), GO5s (black), GO10s (green), GO20s (yellowish green), GO30s (pink), GO50s (dark blue) and (c) plot of concentration vs. transmittance. (d) Weight fraction of salted-out GO at various concentrations and (e) in-series weight fraction of GO. (f) Raman spectra of the D and G bands of GO (black), GO10p (red) GO20p from GO10s (blue), GO50p from GO20s (green) and (g) their high-resolution XPS narrow scans of the C1a region. XPS analysis reveals the presence of C–C (284.5eV) (GO: 39%, GO10p: 46%, GO20p: 40%, GO50p: 34%), C–O (286eV) (GO: 43%, GO10p: 39%, GO20p: 44%, GO50p: 46%), CO (287.3eV) (GO: 12%, GO10p: 11%, GO20p: 12%, GO50p: 13%) and O–COH(288.5eV) (GO: 6%, GO10p: 4%, GO20p: 4%, GO50p: 7%).

Separation of GQDs from chemically exfoliated GO suspensions. (a) TEM images of GQD supernatant from GO50s and (b) high-resolution TEM images of a single GQD. (c) PL spectra (E ex =325) of GO50p (black) and GQD supernatant from GO50s (red) and (d) UV–Vis absorption (Abs), (e) Raman spectra of D and G bands of GO50p (black), and GQD (red) from GO50s and their (f) high-resolution XPS narrow scans. XPS analysis reveals the presence of C–C (284.5eV) (GO50s: 68%), C–O (286eV) (GO50s: 18%), CO (287.3eV) (GO50s: 5%) and O–COH (288.5eV) (GO50s: 9%). (e) UV–Vis absorption (Abs) and (f) PL spectra (E ex =320) of GO50p (black) and GQD supernatant from GO50s (red).

head.ce:title: Salting-out as a scalable, in-series purification method of graphene oxides from microsheets to quantum dots

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Chemically modified graphenes (CMGs) are used to reinforce composites and as platforms for sensing, electrodes in batteries, conductive films for displays, and others. However, performance of CMG materials is often limited by their physicochemical heterogeneity. Here, we report that simple addition of ammonium sulfate to heterogeneous CMG mixtures, results in effective, scalable, and in-series purification, by salting-out precipitation. The mechanism is based on differences in the dispersibility of the individual graphene oxide (GO). At relatively low ammonium sulfate concentrations (∼10mM), large, GOs with predominant hydrophobic domains are precipitated. As the salt concentration increases, smaller and less GOs with predominant hydrophobic domains are purified, and eventually graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are isolated. The method described herein was inspired by a well-known protein purification process called ‘salting-out,’ in which proteins of different sizes and surface charge, successively become indispersible over a narrow range of salt concentrations. The salting-out process for GOs is simple and scalable, and can be used to achieve large-scale purification.

ce:section.ce:para: Salting-out has become an important tool in biology, health science, and related industries [1]. Particularly in the biological sciences, salting-out has been widely implemented to purify proteins. Salting-out is an extremely simple method in which a salt, typically ammonium sulfate, ((NH4)2SO4), is added to an aqueous solution containing a heterogeneous mixture of biological macromolecules. An increase in the salt concentration results in a decrease in the amount of water available to dissolve the heterogeneous biomacromolecules. As the salt concentration increases, molecules with low solubility precipitate first, due to the lack of available water molecules for dissolution [2–4]. The salting-out behavior of a protein at various salt concentrations was first observed by Hofmeister in 1988 [5]. Subsequently, many important proteins have been purified by salting-out processes. One example is the in-series purification of a mixture of aldolase, pyruvate kinase, glyceraldehyde phosphate dehydrogenase, lactate dehydrogenase, and phosphoglycerate mutase using different concentrations of salts [6,7]. Because of its simplicity, the salting-out method is scalable. Therefore, this technique could be adapted to many industrial procedures and scientific protocols.

Chemically modified graphenes (CMG) are similar to proteins in many ways. First, CMGs prepared by chemical intercalation, display size heterogeneity similar to the wide range of molecular weights of proteins. Second, variations in the degree of CMG oxidation result in amphiphilicity caused by the presence of hydrophobic sp2 carbon networks, and hydrophilic polar hydroxyl, carboxyl, and epoxide groups [8–11]. The hydrophobic and polar amino acid residues on protein surfaces similarly result in amphiphilicity that governs solubility. Third, single layer GO exhibits conformational changes like protein folding in responses to its environments such as pH, salinity, or temperature [12,13]. As individual proteins differ in their biological functions and properties, CMGs differ in their electronic and optical behavior depending on their size and chemical functionalities. For example, graphene is a well-known material that exhibits ballistic electronic conduction; however, when the size of graphene particles approaches the Bohr radius, they are called graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [14]. GQD becomes a semiconductor due to quantum confinement [15,16] and edge [17] effects. Therefore, the development of a well-defined, homogeneous purification method is important to the study of graphene science and technology. Because the salting-out strategy has proven effective in purifying proteins from heterogeneous mixtures, we hypothesized that a similar salting-out method could be applied to a heterogeneous CMG suspension. Our hypothesis was based on the aforementioned physicochemical similarities between CMGs and proteins. In fact, recent studies demonstrated that GO flakes undergo intra-molecular foldings caused by existence of hydrophilic and hydrophobic domains [12,13].

Recently, purification methods of graphenes have been reported. Centrifugal purification of graphenes based on their size and/or density [18–20]. Another published approach, capillary electrophoresis, relied on the surface charge of the graphene [21]. Salting-out is a distinctive purification method because it relies on a different variable, the dispersibility of the CMGs, which is closely related to both the material’s physical properties, for example size, and its chemical properties, such as surface charge. In addition, as demonstrated by the successful large-scale purification of numerous proteins, the salting-out method has the critical advantage of full scalability. Another advantage of the salting-out technique is that GQD can be obtained starting from a heterogeneous CMG suspension.

We report here for the first time that the simple addition of a salt to a heterogeneous CMG mixture can be used to effectively purify a series of CMG subpopulations, each of which possesses a narrow window of physicochemical properties. We found that an important subpopulation of CMGs called GQD, generated by a typical modified Hummer’s method, can also be purified using the salting-out technique. Thus, the salting-out purification method described herein could enable the use of graphene in technologies and applications such as batteries, transistors and photovoltaic devices, in which the homogeneity of the graphene dictates the performance of the device.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and suspension of graphene oxide (GO)
ce:section.ce:para: GO was prepared using a modified Hummer’s method. Natural graphite (1g) (SP-1, Bay Carbon, USA) and 40ml H2SO4 (Junsei, Japan), were added to a 250-ml flask at room temperature. The mixture was stirred and cooled to 0°C in an ice bath. Then, 7g KMnO4 (Sigma Aldrich, Korea) was slowly added to the mixture over 5min with stirring. The temperature of the mixture was brought to 35°C in a water bath and maintained for 2h. The reacted mixture was then cooled in an ice bath and diluted by the addition of 200ml water. Until gas evolution ceased, H2O2 (Sigma Aldrich, Korea) was added to the mixture. The GO suspension was filtered through a glass frit filter (medium size) and washed several times with a 10% HCl (Junsei, Japan) solution. Finally, the GO powder was dried in a vacuum at room temperature for 7days.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Separation of GO and GQD from the suspension
ce:section.ce:para: To prepare the suspension, the as-prepared GO (1mg/ml) was dispersed in water using ultrasonication. A 20mM (NH4)2SO4 (Sigma Aldrich, Korea) solution was added to the GO suspension, followed by magnetic stirring at 400rpm for 20min. Prior to separation, the agglomerated GO particles were removed by ultracentrifugation at 10,000rpm for 5min. Then, the salted-out precipitates and the supernatant were separated by ultracentrifugation at 10,000rpm for 5min. An (NH4)2SO4 solution was added to the supernatant beginning at 20mM (NH4)2SO4 and increasing to 50mM over 20min. Once more, the salted-out precipitates and supernatant were separated by ultracentrifugation at 10,000rpm for 5min. The separated supernatant and salted-out precipitates were re-dispersed with water, and the salt was removed by dialysis. The pH of the dialysis solution was increase to pH 8, which has a purpose of forming stable GO colloids through electrostatic stabilization as well as inducing any detachable GO fragments. The aforementioned process was repeated with 10, 20, 30, 40 and 50mM (NH4)2SO4.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Tecnai G F30 S-Twin, FEI, Netherlands) was used to observe the microstructures of the salted-out GOs and GQDs. The sizes of the GOs and GQDs were characterized using atomic force microscopy (AFM) (Nanoman, VEECO, USA). The separated GO and GQD suspensions were directly spin-coated onto a Si wafer, and the sizes of 40 of these prepared specimens were measured using image analysis. To measure the degree of salting-out, ultraviolet–visiblespectrophotometer (UV–Vis) spectrophotometry (HP 8483, Hewlett–Packard, USA) was performed. A range of (NH4)2SO4 concentrations, 0–100mM, was added to the GO suspension (0.2mg/ml). Samples were extracted from the supernatant and the precipitates salted out of the solution for analysis using UV–Vis spectrophotometry. The GO composition in the samples was evaluated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (ESCA 2000, Thermo VG Scientific, England), Micro-Raman spectroscopy (LabRAM HR UV/Vis/NIR, Horiba Jobin Yvon, France), and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) (IFS66V/S & Hyperion 3000, Bruker Optiks, German). The GO samples were directly attached to bare Si wafers for both analyses. A high-resolution micro-photoluminescence system (PL) (LabRAM HR UV/Vis/NIR PL, Horiba Jobin Yvon, France), was used to observe the emission properties of the GQDs. For the PL analysis, the GO and GQD suspensions were prepared directly in quartz tubes after dialysis.

We prepared a colloidal heterogeneous suspension of GO, a widely studied CMG, using a modified Hummer’s method. Fig. 1 a illustrates a salting-out phenomenon observed in GO mixtures when ammonium sulfate is added to the solution. Ammonium sulfate is the most effective salt species in the Hofmeister series [5]. When a salt is added to the GO suspension, the quantity of water molecules available for the dissolution of the GOs decreases rapidly to solvate the
NH
4
+
and
SO
4
2 -
ions generated by the addtion of (NH4)2SO4 (Fig. 1a). Furthermore, addition of salt destabilize the surface charge of GO. GO has mainly electronegativity that keeps the sheets separated by their repulsion forces [22,23]. As a result, GO subpopulations that have low dipersibility began to precipitate at low concentrations of added salt. Likewise, other suspended GO subpopulations precipitated as the concentration of (NH4)2SO4 rose further (Fig. 1b). We found that a significant quantity of GO flakes precipitated at (NH4)2SO4 concentrations less than 10mM. The precipitate and supernatant were separated by centrifugation (10,000rpm for 5min). The separated precipitants were then re-dispersed in water after removing the (NH4)2SO4 by dialysis (2nd and 3rd photos, right column, Fig. 1b). Also, the dialyzed, purified GOs did not suffer from salt contamination. There were no nitrogen peaks observed by XPS from ammonium sulfate adsorption (Fig. S1).

To purify additional sub-populations from the GO mixture, in-series, we subsequently increased the salt concentration. The GO sample precipitated from concentrations under 10mM of the salt, is labeled GO10p, and the supernatant that did not precipitate under this condition is labeled GO10s. Likewise, the precipitate and supernatant separated using a 20mM salt solution are labeled GO20p and GO20s, respectively. Thus, one advantage of the salting-out purification method is that it can be performed in-series.

The size of the GOs in the different samples was investigated using AFM. Distinctive differences in size were observed. Fig. 2 shows size distribution histograms and AFM images for the GO20p (top), GO50p (middle), and GO50s (bottom) fractions. The GO20p sample (Fig. 2a) exhibited a size distribution over three times larger (6.7μm on average, n =40) than that of the GO50p samples (Fig. 2b), 2.2μm on average, n =40). Approximately 7% of the colloidal GO remained in solution even in the presence of high salt (50mM). The GO50s were observed to be smaller than 10nm in size (6.8nm on average, n =40) (Fig. 2c). The difference in size results from the in-series salting-out purification and indicates that the purified GOs exhibit behavior similar to that of proteins when salted-out. In general, large proteins tend to be less dipersible, so they precipitate at low salt concentrations. Similar to proteins, large GOs are purified by salting-out at low salt concentrations, and the size of the precipitated GOs decreases as the concentration of salt increases.

The scale bar at the right indicates the size scale of the salted-out GO20p (red), GO50p from GO20s (blue) and GO50s (green). Typical corresponding size distributions of precipitated (a) GO20p; (b) its AFM image; (c) GO50p from GO20s; (d) its AFM image; (e) GO50s from GO20s after removing GO50p; and (f) its AFM image.summarizes the results of our in-series, salting-out purification at salt concentrations of up to 100mM. As the salt concentration was increased, the color of the supernatant gradually changed from black to light yellow. Therefore, the degree of salting-out could be immediately detected by the change in color of the supernatant. The decrease in color intensity was quantified by measuring the transmittance at 550nm (T550) using an UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Figs. 3 b and c). The T550 was measured at salt concentrations of 5mM to above 50mM, at which no further increase in the transmittance was observed. The quantity of purified GO was also determined. In the batch process, the following quantities were purified from the total GO in the sample suspension (Fig. 3d): 23% GO10p, 46% GO20p, 52% GO30p, 77% GO40p, 93% GO50p and 7% GO50s. During the in-series process, the following quantities were collected (Fig. 3e): 23% GO10p, 23% GO20p, 13% GO30p, 25% GO40p, 16% GO50p and 7% GO50s. This salting-out purification is fully scalable; for example, one gram of GO was fractionated to provide 225.4mg GO10p, 235.5mg GO20p, 130.7mg GO30p, 246.8mg GO40p, 153.2mg GO50p and 71.4mg GO50s (Table S1).

Raman spectroscopy was used to characterize the vibrational modes of the separated GOs (Fig. 3f). The Raman spectrum of the GOs prior salting-out exhibited a D-band at 1350cm−1 and a broad G-band at 1590cm−1 (with an I D/I G ratio of 0.96). After salting-out, the ratios were 0.94 for GO10p, 0.95 for GO20p, and 1.00 for GO50p, showing a gradual increase related to ease of salting-out. Thus, the ratio of 0.96 measured for the heterogeneous GO mixture was the approximate average of the I D/I G of the purified fractions. In addition, the 2D-band shifted from 2690cm−1 for GO10p to 2676cm−1 for GO50p (Fig. S2), while the 2D-band peak of the GO mixture prior to the salting-out procedure was 2681cm−1, and the 2D-band peak for graphite was 2700cm−1 [24]. Thus, a fraction such as GO10p purified at a low salt concentration contains GOs that are similar in their surface properties to graphite, confirming our hypothesis that GOs with predominant hydrophobic domains can be precipitated by the addition of salts.

XPS was used to further analyze the chemical composition of the purified GO surfaces (Fig. 3g) [24,25]. The high-resolution C1s spectra from the salted-out fractions of GOs revealed significant differences in the carbon chemistry. The characteristic hydrophobic C–C peak at 284.5eV was significant (46%) in the GO10p fraction. This peak subsequently decreased as the in-series salting-out purification was performed (Fig. 3g, red). For example, the areas under the C–C curve were 40% for GO20p (blue) and 36% for GO50p (green). In contrast, the C1s peaks connected with hydrophilic atoms (oxygen) such as C–O (286eV), CO (287.3eV), and O–COH (288.5eV) subsequently increased as the in-series, salting-out purification was performed. This result is also consistent with the previous characterizations in which a GO with predominant hydrophobic domains subpopulation precipitated earlier than the GO with predominant hydrophilic domains subpopulations. GOs with higher oxygen content are expected to have a high degree of hydration (i.e., high dipersibility), making them resistant to salting-out. For the heterogeneous GOs prior to salting-out, the C–C content was 39%. FTIR analysis confirmed the presence of oxygen functionalities in the salting-out GOs: C–O stretching at 1070cm−1, C–OH stretching at 1220cm−1, O-H deformation at 1440cm−1, CO stretching vibrations at 1720cm−1, and O–H stretching vibrations at 3400cm−1 (Fig. S3). The zeta-potential value of the GOs as a function of (NH4)2SO4 remained strongly negative (−25mV), but exhibited a slight increase at a high concentration of the salt (−17mV for 50mM). This data indicates that the salt added has little influence on the chemistry of the suspended GOs (Fig. S4).

The principle of salting-out predicts that highly water-dipersible GOs with extremely small sizes can be purified; therefore, we expected that GQDs could be isolated from the heterogeneous mixture. In fact, the purified GO fraction GO50s contained GQD. TEM images indicated that small GOs (∼6nm) were isolated (Fig. 4 a and b), and the high-resolution TEM image (Fig. 4b) confirmed the dimensions of the GQDs. As expected, strong fluorescence from the GQDs was measured at a wavelength of ∼580nm (Fig. 4c, red). In contrast, the precipitated fraction of GO50p did not exhibit such fluorescence (Fig. 4c, black). Prior to salting-out, the GO solution did not exhibit this emission peak even in the presence of the GQDs; this might be due to fluorescence quenching caused by interactions between the non-fluorescent, large-sized GOs (Fig. S5). Considering the emission peaks previously reported for GQDs, those obtained from this GO suspension were red-shifted compared with peaks from other GQD fabrication methods [26–30]. Due to their size, the presence of zigzag sites, the fraction of their sp2 domains and their surface functional groups [31–33], we confirmed that most of the GQDs were fabricated during the chemical exfoliation process and could be isolated from the suspension.

GQDs possess a stable, planar high-density sp2 structure compared with GOs present in the GO50p sample, as evidenced by the existence of a characteristic, detectable absorption peak in the range of 300–320nm (λ max =305nm) (red, Fig. 4d). Absorption in the near UV region typically indicates the presence of sp2 orbital resonance. However, no such absorption was detected for the GO50p fraction, indicating a larger contribution of the sp3 orbitals (black). Additional evidence can be found in the lower I D/I G ratio in the Raman spectra. The Raman spectrum of the GQDs exhibited an I D/I G ratio of 0.95, which is lower than that of 1.00 for the GO50p. This result confirms the high density of sp2 orbitals on the plane of the purified GQDs. The 2D-band peak for GQD (2692cm−1) was also close to 2700cm−1, a wavelength indicative of graphite structures (Fig. S6). In the GO50p, the 2D-band appeared at 2676cm−1, further from that expected for graphite structures.

Due to the dominant presence of sp2 orbitals, the oxygen content of the GQDs is expected to be low. The high-resolution C1s spectra from XPS exhibited significant hydrophobic C–C character (68%) with a peak at 284.5eV for the GQDs (Fig. 4f). This high C–C content observed in the GQDs explains the molecular mechanism of the fluorescence emission from the sp2 orbital. Thus, the dipersibility of the GQDs relies on the chemistry at the edges of the particle, where the hydrophilic oxygen-containing functional groups exist.

In this study, we demonstrated that salting out, a purification strategy widely used in biology, is a useful, scalable method for purifying GO. Based on the concentration of ammonium sulfate, a GO subpopulation with a narrow range of physicochemical properties was precipitated, and the precipitant was purified using centrifugation. GOs with predominant hydrophobic domains were salted-out from a mixture at a low concentration of ammonium sulfate, and GO with predominant hydrophilic domains subpopulations were subsequently salted-out in series as the salt concentration was increased. Furthermore, the GO subpopulation that remained in the suspension at high salt concentrations (>50mM) was characterized as GQDs (<10nm). This in-series, scalable purification method, using salting-out, is promising for the potential development of a variety of graphene-associated devices in which physicochemically homogeneous graphenes are required.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support from the National Research Foundation of South Korea: World Class University Program (R31-10071, H.L.), Molecular-level Interface Research Center (2012-0000909, H.L.), and Korea Biotech R&D Program (2011K000809, H.L.). This research was supported by the NSL (National Space Lab.) program through the Korea Science and Engineering Foundation funded by the Ministry of Education, Science and Technology (2008-2003213, S.H.H) and the Inha University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2013.06.037.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Critical appraisal of salting-out and its implications for chemical and biological sciences

The doctrine of the action of the salts

Solution phase production of graphene with controlled thickness via density differentiation
High-resolution separation of graphene oxide by capillary electrophoresis

Processable aqueous dispersions of graphene nanosheets
